assessment.summary: A recurrent, well-quantified hazard — about 15 magnitude-7 and one magnitude-8+ earthquake expected globally each year — whose worst instances have killed hundreds of thousands of people in a single event.
assessment.timeframe: Large earthquakes recur every year worldwide; a single catastrophic event striking a major population center can occur at any time without reliable short-term prediction.

claims.text: According to long-term records since about 1900, USGS expects about 16 major earthquakes worldwide in any given year, including 15 earthquakes in the magnitude 7 range and one earthquake of magnitude 8.0 or greater.
USGS worldwide earthquake statistics record 298,101 deaths from earthquakes in 2004 (the Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami) and 226,050 in 2010, among the deadliest years on record.

description: A large-magnitude earthquake striking a densely populated or poorly prepared region can kill tens to hundreds of thousands of people in seconds and collapse infrastructure across a wide area. Large earthquakes occur every year somewhere on Earth; the risk is concentrated along active plate boundaries such as the Pacific Ring of Fire.
name: Major Earthquake
